answer:
**"Ślokāntara" is the title of a single-chapter Medieval Kawi (Old Javanese) prose work that systematically quotes and glosses 84 (or more) Sanskrit *ślokas* (verses) on *dharma* (righteousness), rebirth, social duties, flaws/virtues, sins, and cosmology.**[^1][^2]

The title opens the text and is repeated at the close: **Ślokāntara** (literally "verse-other/interior/difference"). It denotes a collection of miscellaneous or "other" *ślokas* drawn eclectically from Sanskrit sources (e.g., on *varṇas*, *yugas*, *pātakas*), each followed by a Kawi prose gloss (*kaliṅanya* = "the meaning is").[^3][^4]

The term **"śloka"** (defined in the query as "verse, Sanskrit unit of metrical composition") appears **literally once** in the English translation of the commentary on śloka 61:[^5]

> A person who interprets **the meaning of a *śloka*** should not say that their understanding of the holy knowledge is complete. Even if the meaning of the scripture is multiplied a thousand times, one should still suspect that there is yet another meaning to the holy teachings.[^6]

This self-reflexive usage underscores the text's form: terse Sanskrit *ślokas* (e.g., [Slo_61s]) followed by elaborate Kawi explanations ([Slo_61j]). No other verbatim "sloka"/*śloka*/ślo- hits occur in the English body.[^7]

[^1]: `unknown-slokantara-d7a628`, full work metadata and chapter list.
[^2]: [Chapter 1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/), *Slokantara*, Ch. 1 `citation_url`.
[^3]: [p-aa8078](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-aa8078).
[^4]: [p-404472](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-404472).
[^5]: Search-limited to English *translation* variant body; no matches in *transliteration* (raw Kawi).
[^6]: [p-02783f](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-02783f).
[^7]: Exhaustive tool scans (e.g., `search_corpus("sloka")`, `search_corpus("\"śloka\"")`) returned zero beyond this; *ślokas* are implicit throughout as quoted verse units.
